---
title: "Lab 7: Drug Design (Molecular Docking)"
format: html
engine: knitr
filters:
  - webr
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, results='asis'}
library(webexercises)
```

## From Structure to Medicine — target: MDM2

Having the predicted 3D structure of a protein (thanks to AlphaFold from Lab 5), we can move on to **Rational Drug Design**. Instead of testing millions of chemical compounds "blindly" in the laboratory, we use computer simulations.

The most popular method is **Molecular Docking** — fitting a key (a drug molecule, the ligand) into a lock (the binding pocket of our receptor protein).

The docking algorithm evaluates the **Binding Affinity**. The more negative the score (e.g., -9.0 kcal/mol), the stronger the drug binds to the protein!

::: {.callout-note}
## Why MDM2?
In Lab 6, we saw that **MDM2** is overexpressed in osteosarcoma. MDM2 binds TP53 and blocks its tumour-suppressor function. Blocking MDM2 with a small molecule restores TP53 activity — a real therapeutic strategy under investigation in clinical trials with drugs like **Nutlin-3** and **RG7112**.
:::

### Interactive Task: MDM2 Binding Pocket Geometry

Before docking, the algorithm needs the coordinates of the binding pocket centre. We use real data from the MDM2 crystal structure in the PDB (**4HBM**: MDM2 with a small-molecule inhibitor).

Key residues lining the p53-binding cleft of MDM2: **L26**, **F55**, **M62**, **Y67**, **V75**.

```{webr-r}
# X, Y, Z coordinates of key MDM2 binding pocket residues (PDB: 4HBM)
# These residues line the p53-binding cleft of MDM2
residues <- c("L26", "F55", "M62", "Y67", "V75")
x_coords <- c(13.2, 15.8, 14.5, 16.2, 13.9)
y_coords <- c(24.8, 26.1, 23.7, 25.3, 22.8)
z_coords <- c( 7.1,  9.4,  8.9,  7.5,  9.2)

# Calculate the binding pocket center (mean of coordinates)
center_x <- mean(x_coords)
center_y <- mean(y_coords)
center_z <- mean(z_coords)

cat("MDM2 binding pocket center (Box Center for AutoDock Vina):\n")
cat("X:", round(center_x, 2), "\n")
cat("Y:", round(center_y, 2), "\n")
cat("Z:", round(center_z, 2), "\n")

# Display residue positions
pocket_df <- data.frame(Residue=residues, X=x_coords, Y=y_coords, Z=z_coords)
print(pocket_df)
```

---

## The Finale: Docking Nutlin-3 into MDM2 in the Cloud

Nutlin-3 was one of the first selective MDM2 inhibitors. It occupies the same pocket that TP53 normally uses, blocking their interaction. We will dock it into the MDM2 structure using **AutoDock Vina**.

1. Go to the [PDB](https://www.rcsb.org/) and download structure **4HBM** (MDM2 with ligand). Remove the ligand from the file before docking (keep only the protein).
2. Go to [PubChem](https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/) and search for **Nutlin-3** (CID: 9903051). Download the 3D structure in SDF format.
3. Open the [Google Colab AutoDock Vina notebook](https://colab.research.google.com/github/AngelRuizMoreno/Jupyter_Dock/blob/main/docking_notebooks/SingleDocking.ipynb).
4. Upload the protein and ligand files, enter the pocket coordinates calculated above, and run the simulation.
5. Record the binding affinity (kcal/mol) and compare with other ligands.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Expected result
Nutlin-3 binds MDM2 with an affinity of approximately **-8 to -10 kcal/mol** — strong binding, consistent with its nanomolar biological activity.
:::

---

## Course Summary: From Sequence to Drug

Over seven labs we completed a full bioinformatics pipeline — from a single sequence to virtual drug design — using **osteosarcoma** as a consistent clinical thread:

| Lab | What we did | Osteosarcoma connection |
|-----|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Databases & APIs | Fetching TP53/RB1 sequences |
| 2 | Alignment algorithms | TP53: human vs. mouse vs. dog |
| 3 | Profiles & BLAST | TP53 mutation hotspot logo |
| 4 | NGS & variant calling | VCF with R248W, R273H mutations |
| 5 | Phylogenetics & AlphaFold | TP53 tree, 3D structure with hotspots |
| 6 | RNA-Seq | Volcano Plot: MDM2 overexpressed |
| 7 | Molecular Docking | Nutlin-3 blocks MDM2, restores TP53 |

---

## Knowledge Check

**Question 1:** What do we call the small molecule (e.g., a drug) that we try to fit into a large protein?
`r mcq(c("Receptor", answer = "Ligand", "Reference sequence"))`

**Question 2:** When evaluating docking results (Binding Affinity), which score suggests a **stronger** and more stable bond between the drug and the protein?
`r mcq(c("-2.5 kcal/mol", answer = "-9.8 kcal/mol", "+1.2 kcal/mol"))`
